title: What the Body Does in the Doctor's Waiting Room
dek: *The fluorescent hum, the vinyl chairs, the clipboard forms—your nervous system reads the waiting room as a place where you must be small, compliant, and patient. But your body remembers every dismissal, every time you were told your pain was normal, every appointment that started forty minutes late. Here is what happens in the flesh while you sit still and wait.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Your body begins preparing for threat the moment you walk into a medical waiting room, tightening muscles and raising heart rate even when you feel calm. This autonomic response often masks suppressed anger about being kept waiting, dismissed, or reduced to a number on a chart. Learning to notice these physical signals helps you reclaim agency in a system designed to make you passive.

She arrived twelve minutes early because that is what the automated reminder told her to do. Now she has been sitting for thirty-seven minutes, phone battery at 11%, watching the receptionist eat yogurt behind plexiglass. Her shoulders have migrated up toward her ears. Her jaw aches on the left side. She does not feel angry. She feels tired, maybe a little anxious about the blood work, but certainly not angry. When the nurse finally calls her name, she will smile and say "no problem at all" when apologized to. She will not notice that her hands have been in fists. ## The Autonomic Rehearsal Before the Door Opens Your body starts its performance before you even enter the building. In my own practice, I have watched people describe the physical sensations that begin in the parking lot: a subtle nausea, a need to urinate, a heaviness in the limbs. This is not hypochondria. This is your nervous system reading environmental cues and preparing you for an encounter it has learned to categorize as potentially invalidating. The autonomic nervous system does not wait for conscious permission. It samples the air for threat: the antiseptic smell, the particular quality of institutional lighting, the muffled sounds of suffering behind closed doors. Peter Levine's work on trauma and the body emphasizes that the nervous system maintains a continuous scan for safety or danger, often triggering defensive responses long before the thinking brain catches up. You may believe you are simply "going to the doctor," but your body is already armoring. This armoring shows up as shallow breathing, a tightening across the chest, a slight increase in heart rate. The muscles of your face may go deliberately neutral, a learned blankness that says *I am compliant, I will not be difficult, please help me*. And underneath that blankness, a small hot coal of resentment that you have trained yourself not to feel. The waiting room is designed to make you wait, and waiting is a power dynamic. You cannot leave. You cannot demand attention. You cannot know when your name will be called. Your body knows this, even when your mind insists it is fine. ## What the Throat Does When You Swallow Your Words There is a constriction that happens at the base of the throat when anger is not allowed to move. You might notice it as a need to clear your throat repeatedly, or as a sensation of something stuck, a thickness that makes swallowing feel deliberate. This is not psychosomatic in the dismissive sense. This is the physical reality of words you have decided not to say. Beverly Engel, in her research on the relationship between shame and anger, points out that many people learn to convert anger into self-directed criticism or bodily symptoms rather than risk expressing it outward. The throat becomes a holding place for the protest that is too dangerous to voice: *Why am I always kept waiting? Why does no one explain anything? Why do I have to beg to be believed?* In our experience, people who spend years in medical settings - chronic illness, caretaking, repeated procedures - develop what we might call a "compliant throat." The vocal cords do not fully engage. The voice comes out smaller, higher, apologetic. This is a survival adaptation. Doctors have power. Nurses control access. Receptionists guard the gate. Anger, in this context, feels like a risk you cannot afford. But the body does not forget what the throat refused to say. That constriction becomes habitual. It shows up in other contexts: at work, in relationships, anywhere you perceive an authority you cannot challenge. Over time, the muscle memory of swallowing anger becomes the baseline, and you stop noticing you are doing it. ## The Hands and What They Grip Look at your hands the next time you are waiting. Are they folded? Clenched? Wrapped around your phone like a talisman? The hands are often the first place we can observe unfelt anger, because they prepare for action even when action is forbidden. Robert Augustus Masters writes about the embodied experience of shadow emotions, noting that anger often first appears as tension in the hands and forearms, a readiness to push away or strike that gets frozen mid-gesture. In the waiting room, your hands may grip the armrests, dig into your thighs, or twist a tissue into damp shreds. You are not conscious of doing this. You would be surprised if someone pointed it out. The hands want to do something. They want to knock on the receptionist's window and ask why this is taking so long. They want to crumple the intake form that asks the same questions you answered last month. They want to push open the door and leave. But you have been socialized to keep your hands still, to be patient, to wait your turn like a good patient. This frozen action lives in the muscles. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to the idea that unexpressed anger does not evaporate - it takes up residence in tissue, in fascia, in the small repetitive movements we do not notice. The hands remember every time they wanted to act and were told to stay still. Some people find that [Banyan Botanicals Gotu Kola Powder (3.5 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008X9JM68?tag=spankyspinola-20), taken as a gentle daily tea, helps calm the jittery restlessness that accumulates in the extremities, though it is no substitute for actually letting your hands express what they need to express. ## The Legs That Cannot Leave Your legs are built for locomotion, for moving toward what you want and away from what harms you. But in the waiting room, they are pinned. You cannot leave without forfeiting the appointment you may have waited months to get. You cannot pace without drawing attention. You cannot run. This immobilization triggers a very old mammalian response. When fight and flight are both impossible, the body begins to shut down into a freeze state. You might notice your legs going numb, a pins-and-needles sensation, or a strange heaviness as though they belong to someone else. This is not poor circulation. This is your nervous system trying to protect you from the distress of being trapped. The anger that lives in the legs is the anger of thwarted agency. *I should be able to leave. I should not have to tolerate this. My time matters.* But the system is designed so that your time does not matter, or matters less than efficiency, less than the physician's schedule, less than the insurance protocols that dictate how many minutes you are worth. When you finally stand to follow the nurse back, your legs may feel unsteady. You might stumble slightly, laugh it off, blame it on sitting too long. But your body has been rehearsing captivity, and it takes a moment to remember how to move freely again. ## The Belly and the Breath You Are Not Taking Anger needs oxygen. It needs the full expansion of the lungs, the engagement of the diaphragm, the kind of breath that fills the belly and makes space for feeling. But in the waiting room, most people breathe only into the upper chest, shallow sips of air that keep them just oxygenated enough to stay conscious but not enough to feel fully alive. This shallow breathing is part of the freeze response, but it is also a learned behavior. Deep breathing draws attention to the body, and drawing attention to the body in a medical setting can feel dangerous. What if they notice you are anxious? What if they write "patient appears agitated" in your chart? What if your rapid heartbeat gets attributed to anxiety instead of the thyroid condition you have been trying to get them to test for? So you breathe small. You make yourself small. And the anger that needs breath to move stays locked in the belly, a hot stone of resentment that you will carry home and perhaps not recognize until hours later when you snap at someone you love over something trivial. A simple practice: While you wait, place one hand on your belly. Do not try to change your breath. Just notice it. Count four seconds on the inhale, four seconds on the exhale. Do this for three minutes. You are not trying to calm down. You are trying to come back into your body, to feel what is actually happening rather than what you have been trained to perform. If anger surfaces, let it. You do not have to do anything with it. Just let it be present. The research compiled in the NIH overview of anger and the autonomic nervous system confirms what your body already knows: suppressed anger disrupts normal breathing patterns and maintains a state of physiological arousal that your conscious mind may not register as distress. ## The Face You Are Making Without Knowing It Your face is performing. In the waiting room, it performs patience, pleasantness, the absence of demands. This performance is so automatic that you do not realize you are doing it until your jaw starts to ache or you catch your reflection in the bathroom mirror and see the tightness around your eyes. The muscles of the face are intimately connected to emotional expression. When you suppress anger, you suppress the facial movements that would naturally accompany it: the furrowed brow, the tightened jaw, the flared nostrils. Instead, you might hold a small, neutral smile, a mask of calm that costs you more energy than you realize. Some people develop tension headaches that begin at the temples and wrap around the skull. Some grind their teeth at night and wake with sore jaws. Some notice a tic, a small repetitive movement - blinking, lip-biting, nose-wrinkling - that their body does to discharge the tension that their face will not fully express. Over the years I have seen people slowly reclaim their faces by practicing what we might call "private expression." In your car, before you go in, let your face make the expression it wants to make. Furrow your brow. Bare your teeth. Scrunch your whole face into a knot of frustration. This is not childish. This is letting the muscles do what they are designed to do, so they do not have to hold that tension for the next hour while you perform pleasantness. ## When the Body Keeps Score in the Chair The waiting room chair is rarely comfortable. It is vinyl or hard plastic, designed for easy cleaning rather than ease. Your body conforms to it, or tries to, shifting every few minutes in search of a position that does not hurt. But the discomfort is not only about the chair. Your body is holding a posture of readiness, a subtle bracing against the anticipated interaction. You are not relaxed. You cannot relax, because relaxation in this context feels like letting your guard down, and you have learned that letting your guard down in medical settings can mean being dismissed, doubted, or overlooked. The chronic muscle tension that develops from repeated waiting - in doctors' offices, in emergency rooms, in specialists' lobbies - becomes a kind of body memory. Your shoulders learn to hunch. Your lower back learns to ache. Your hips learn to tighten. And because these sensations are constant, you stop noticing them as anger and start experiencing them as "just how my body is." Some people find [Solgar 5-HTP 100 mg (alt SKU)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z94138?tag=spankyspinola-20) helpful for the low-grade anxiety that often accompanies this kind of chronic tension, though it works best alongside practices that help you actually feel and move the anger rather than simply dampening it chemically. If you are interested in exploring more about how the body stores emotional patterns, [the assessments hub](/assessments) offers several tools for mapping your own somatic landscape. ## The Practice of the Five-Minute Inventory Here is something you can do, either in the waiting room itself or afterward as a way of processing what happened in your body: Set a timer for five minutes. Write or speak aloud (voice memo on your phone works well) a continuous stream answering this prompt: "My body wanted to _____ but I did not let it because _____." Do not edit. Do not make it pretty. Let the sentences be fragments. Let them repeat. Let them be petty or dramatic or embarrassing. *My body wanted to walk out but I did not let it because I need this referral. My body wanted to yell at the receptionist but I did not let it because I would be labeled difficult. My body wanted to cry but I did not let it because crying makes people uncomfortable.* This is not about changing anything. This is about making conscious the gap between impulse and action, between what your body knows it needs and what your socialization permits. In that gap lives your anger, and bringing it into awareness is the first step toward deciding what to do with it. You might be surprised by what emerges. You might find anger at the medical system, at yourself, at the person who told you your symptoms were not serious, at the years you spent trying to be the good patient who never complained. Let it all come. The timer will hold the container. Five minutes is long enough to get past the polite surface and short enough that it will not consume your day. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Is it normal to feel physically tense in medical settings even when nothing is wrong? Absolutely. Your nervous system does not distinguish between current threat and remembered threat. If you have had previous experiences of being dismissed, misdiagnosed, or kept waiting excessively, your body will prepare for that possibility every time you enter a similar environment. This is adaptive, not pathological. The question is whether you are aware of it and whether it is serving you or simply exhausting you. ### What if I do not feel angry at all, just tired or numb? Numbness and fatigue are often what anger looks like when it has been suppressed for a long time. The body requires energy to hold down emotion, and that energy expenditure registers as exhaustion. If you find yourself consistently tired after medical appointments, even brief ones, consider whether there might be unfelt anger underneath the fatigue. Sometimes what we call depression is actually anger that has been turned inward for so long we no longer recognize it. ### Can these physical patterns change, or am I stuck with them? They can absolutely change, but change requires awareness and practice. Your body learned these patterns because they were useful at some point - they kept you safe, kept you compliant, kept you from being labeled difficult. Unlearning them means finding new ways to be safe while also honoring your authentic responses. This is slow work, but it is possible. The resources at [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) can support the process, as can somatic therapies, but the core work is learning to trust your body's signals rather than overriding them. ## What Happens After You Leave You walk out into the parking lot, prescription in hand or referral slip or another appointment card for three months from now. The sun is too bright. Your body feels strange, like you are inhabiting it from a slight distance. You might feel relief that it is over, or dread about coming back, or a vague dissatisfaction you cannot name. This is the moment when the anger often surfaces, now that you are out of the context that required you to suppress it. You might find yourself irritated at the slow driver in front of you, snapping at the pharmacy tech, slamming the car door harder than necessary. Or you might go numb, scroll your phone for an hour, eat something you do not taste, and only later - days later, maybe - realize you are furious. The body does not forget what happened in the waiting room. It holds the score, as the saying goes, until you are ready to feel it. And when you are ready, when you can sit with the anger instead of performing patience, something shifts. You remember that your time matters. That your pain is real. That you do not have to be small to deserve care. The waiting room taught your body to contract, but you can teach it, slowly, to expand again. You can learn, as we explore more deeply at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) and throughout [the about page](/about), that anger is not the enemy of healing - it is often the beginning. For those moments when impatience itself becomes overwhelming, some find [Bach Original Flower Remedies, Impatiens (Non-Alcohol Formula) - Have Patience -](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BM4XTD92?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a gentle recalibration, though the deeper work is learning what your impatience is trying to tell you. Your body in the waiting room is not malfunctioning. It is speaking. The question is whether you are willing to listen. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on what the body does in the doctor's waiting room, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
